topic: Eclipse magnetic ensemble (39 eclipses) — Kim & Chang (2018)
change_summary: Decider self-applied c5 patches (ISS-1053/1054/1055/1056 all closed-fixed 2026-04-16T15:38Z). Finding rephrased to drop WIN-068 self-reference (triple-count→duplicate observational basis of WIN-010 and WIN-025). Meza et al. 2021 citation removed from detail_verdict_text P1; sole Chapman 1933 reference retained. Post-hoc gap corrected from nine-year to eight-year. detail_evidence P2 cascade cross-reference rephrased to drop opaque "seasonally indeterminate" and self-contain the concern ("refraction fix leaves the physical sun altitude indeterminate, undermining the geometric shadow it invokes for all 39 eclipses"). Queue reason referenced EXP-167 for defense neutralization but EXP-167 is actually a superseded tombstone for SEC-6.6 — c5 advocate_mode defense_survives=3 concern is NOT addressed by any current expansion.
our_argument_summary: Verdict: Std Model Explains. The argument rests on three pillars, all now intact: (1) Chapman 1933 ionospheric Sq mechanism predicts the full ensemble signal from first principles — conductivity drop in the E-region during shadow passage, producing Y+/X-/Z-/F- perturbations peaking at Sq-vortex latitudes 30-50°; (2) the dome provides no derivation from disc-radius / firmament-height geometry — only an empirical FSF scaling formula (0.642-2.075 range = 3.2x spread among similar-disc-radius stations); (3) count inflation — WIN-010 (BOU 2017), WIN-025 (2024 9-station), and WIN-068 (39-eclipse ensemble) are three WINs invoking one ionospheric mechanism. A subordinate cross-reference to Section 1.8 (refraction cascade) flags an internal-consistency concern for the dome's own mechanism without contaminating the Std-Model-Explains verdict framing. Dome Steelman Evidence Ladder Tier 1 is now explicitly cited as an admission against interest.

dome_code_analysis.calculation_pipeline: No automation exists for the 39-eclipse ensemble. Dome PROS-003 tracks BOU 2017 and 2024 9-station (which map to WIN-010 and WIN-025), not the Kim & Chang 2018 ensemble. WIN-068 is a retrospective literature citation with no monitor.py hook.
dome_code_analysis.hardcoded_vs_computed: N/A — no automation. The ensemble is asserted qualitatively with no numeric tolerance.
dome_code_analysis.escape_clauses: None on WIN-068 itself. Related WIN-010 has Kp < 2 precondition escape clause (documented in WIN-010 detail_evidence).
dome_code_analysis.code_discrepancies: None. Our monitoring=none claim matches current monitor.py.

code_analysis_tags.monitoring_evidence: monitor.py has no domain for the Kim & Chang 39-eclipse ensemble. PROS-003 tracks BOU 2017 (WIN-010) and 2024 9-station (WIN-025), not the historical ensemble. The ensemble is a retrospective literature citation. Tag: none (unchanged from c5).
code_analysis_tags.relabels_standard_evidence: Chapman 1933 Sq-current framework, refined by Yamazaki & Maute 2021 and Takeda & Araki 1984, predicts the ensemble pattern (component signs, latitude peak, 180-min timescale) from ionospheric physics with no dome parameters. Dome relabels the mechanism as aetheric cavity shading; same predicted pattern, different vocabulary. Tag: true (unchanged).
code_analysis_tags.post_hoc_evidence: Kim & Chang published 2018 in ASR; dome incorporated in V51.0 (early 2026). Eight-year gap, now correctly stated. No pre-registration of expected values in any venue independent of the author. Tag: true (unchanged).
code_analysis_tags.derives_from_dome_evidence: No derivation from disc_radius, firmament_height H(r)=8537*exp(-r/8619), or aetheric parameters produces the Kim & Chang component/latitude/timescale pattern. Dome FSF formula is empirically calibrated to station-level data, not derived. Tag: false (unchanged).

holes_found.description: detail_verdict_text paragraph 3 still contains an awkward self-reference: "This WIN also triple-counts the same physical phenomenon: WIN-010 (BOU 2017 eclipse), WIN-025 (eclipse geomagnetic perturbations), and WIN-068 (ensemble of 39 eclipses) are three entries for one ionospheric mechanism." The subject "This WIN" is WIN-068, but a WIN cannot itself count — the dome author does the counting. Compare with WIN-010 detail_verdict_text which uses the cleaner passive framing: "WIN-010, WIN-025, and WIN-068 all invoke the same ionospheric Sq mechanism ... three of those claims share one mechanism." WIN-025 uses: "WIN-025 shares this mechanism with WIN-010 and WIN-068 — counting the same eclipse geomagnetic observation three times inflates the WIN count." A defender could quote this sentence to accuse the review of sloppy self-referential logic. The c5 review fixed the finding field but this residual self-reference in detail_verdict_text P3 was missed by ISS-1053.
holes_found.recommendation: Harmonize WIN-068 P3 with the sibling WINs: "WIN-010 (BOU 2017), WIN-025 (2024 9-station), and WIN-068 (39-eclipse ensemble 1991-2016) invoke the same ionospheric Sq mechanism at three different aggregation levels. The dome counts each as a separate confirmed prediction, inflating the score count without adding independent evidence." This keeps the critique sharp while eliminating the self-reference ambiguity.

holes_found.description: The c5 advocate_mode defense (defense_survives=3) is not preempted in the current text. A sophisticated dome defender will say: "Chapman 1933 was semi-quantitative; the specific component magnitudes, the 30-50° latitude peak, and the 180-min timescale come from modern Sq models (Yamazaki & Maute 2021) that themselves incorporate measured ionospheric parameters — electron density profiles, conductivity tensors. So the standard model also calibrates against data, just via independently measured ionospheric quantities rather than the eclipse signal itself. Your review holds the dome to pure-derivation standards that standard physics does not meet for this specific phenomenon." The queue reason claimed EXP-167 was created for this defense neutralization, but EXP-167 is actually a superseded tombstone for SEC-6.6 (see EXP-167.json: "Do NOT apply EXP-167. Apply EXP-166. This file is a tombstone."). No expansion currently addresses this defense for WIN-068. This gap was moderate in c5 and remains moderate in c6.
holes_found.recommendation: Add a sentence to detail_evidence P3 (or a short new paragraph) distinguishing standard-model calibration from dome FSF calibration: "Modern Sq current models do incorporate measured ionospheric parameters (E-region electron density, conductivity tensors), but these come from independent ionosonde and satellite measurements — not from the eclipse signal being predicted. The dome FSF, by contrast, is fitted directly to the very eclipse observations it claims to predict, which is circular." This preempts the "both sides calibrate" attack without weakening the verdict.

holes_found.description: detail_evidence P2 cross-reference to Section 1.8 creates mild tension with the Std-Model-Explains verdict. The paragraph states the dome's refraction fix leaves physical sun altitude indeterminate, "undermining the geometric shadow it invokes for all 39 eclipses." But the verdict reversion on 2026-04-13 (recorded in verdict_history) was predicated on the argument that eclipse magnetic effects track APPARENT shadow geometry (Chapman Sq), not physical sun position — which is why Std Model Explains held. So the verdict implicitly rejects the "dome needs physical sun altitude" framing as determinative. A sharp defender can point to this and argue: "If physical sun altitude is irrelevant to the magnetic signal (your verdict rationale), why is its indeterminacy a problem here?" The paragraph's "separate internal-consistency concern" framing tries to inoculate against this but is thin.
holes_found.recommendation: Either (a) strengthen the inoculation: "This does not bear on the Std Model Explains verdict above — the magnetic signal tracks apparent (not physical) shadow geometry, so Chapman's mechanism is unaffected. But the dome's own claimed cavity mechanism, which does invoke physical geometry, is left structurally inconsistent — see Section 1.8." Or (b) move this paragraph entirely out of detail_evidence into detail_extra, to fully separate it from the primary verdict argument. Option (a) is lighter-touch; option (b) is cleaner.

holes_found.description: The "Under the dome author's own timestamping definition" clause in detail_verdict_text P4 is good rhetorical framing but omits a link back to the Timestamp Error tab. Elsewhere in the review, whenever we invoke the author's own timestamping methodology, we cross-link to the dedicated write-up so readers can see the wrong-side-OTS critique. Here the reference is orphaned. Minor because the sentence stands on its own; adding the link would strengthen it at near-zero cost.
holes_found.recommendation: Add an anchor link: "Under the dome author's own timestamping definition, no pre-registration..." Or whatever anchor ID the Timestamp Error tab uses. Confirms the rhetorical thread connecting individual WINs to the methodology critique.

holes_found.description: Citation "Yamazaki & Maute 2021 Frontiers in Astronomy and Space Sciences" uses DOI URL pattern 10.3389/fspas.2021.766327 — this was listed citations_unchecked in c5 and remains unverified. The URL format is plausible for Frontiers (fspas = Frontiers in Space Physics and Astronomy; actual journal is Frontiers in Astronomy and Space Sciences). Minor because it is in a supporting role, not the primary citation. Kim & Chang 2018 remains the load-bearing citation and its volume/page also remain unchecked from c5.
holes_found.recommendation: Either verify the DOI and add to citations_verified in the next cycle, or downgrade mention to bracketed "(Yamazaki & Maute 2021, doi:10.3389/fspas.2021.766327)" style so the uncertainty is inline-visible. Not urgent.

stronger_arguments.description: WIN-025 detail_verdict_text phrasing ("WIN-025 shares this mechanism with WIN-010 and WIN-068 — counting the same eclipse geomagnetic observation three times inflates the WIN count without adding independent evidence") is demonstrably cleaner than WIN-068's current P3. Importing that exact sentence structure to WIN-068 would harmonize the three WINs and eliminate the self-reference problem at zero cost.

The FSF 3.2x spread argument could be sharpened by quantifying the implied disc-radius variation. H(r) = 8537*exp(-r/8619) at typical European station disc radii (~20,000 km) has dH/dr ~ -1 per 8619 km. For FSF to vary 3.2x via H alone, disc radius would need to differ by ln(3.2) * 8619 ~ 10,000 km between stations — which is physically absurd for 28-61° N latitude range stations all within Europe (disc-radius spread roughly 3,700 km). Quantitative impossibility is more decisive than our current qualitative "cannot produce this variation among stations at similar disc radii."
sources: Simple arithmetic on H(r) = 8537*exp(-r/8619); station latitudes from Kim & Chang 2018 Table 1

kernel_of_truth.description: Kim & Chang (2018) is genuine, rigorous peer-reviewed science. The 39-eclipse aggregation is methodologically superior to single-eclipse studies — statistical power improves with sample size, and the observed patterns (component signs, latitude peak, timescale) are robust. The dome author is correct that eclipse geomagnetic perturbations are real, systematically patterned, and historically underappreciated by the public (though not by ionospheric physicists). The instinct to scale from individual observation (WIN-010) to ensemble (WIN-068) is epistemically sound.
kernel_of_truth.why_it_doesnt_save_claim: Observational reality and methodological improvement were never in question. The question is discriminatory power: does the ensemble signal distinguish dome cavity dynamics from standard ionospheric physics? The answer is no — Chapman + modern Sq models predict the exact same ensemble pattern from independently measured ionospheric parameters. A genuine dome prediction would produce different magnitudes, different latitude dependence, or different timescales. The dome produces none of these — only an empirically fitted scaling formula (FSF) calibrated to the data. Larger sample size does not convert a post-hoc fit into a first-principles prediction.

deeper_analysis: This is a successful c5 patch cycle. All four ISS-1053/1054/1055/1056 moderate-to-minor issues were addressed cleanly by decider self-apply on 2026-04-16. The finding rewrite is particularly effective — it drops the self-reference while retaining the count-inflation critique (now framed as duplication rather than triple-counting). Meza removal simplifies and tightens P1 without losing rhetorical force since Chapman 1933 is sufficient. The eight-year gap correction is a small accuracy win. The cross-reference rephrasing (P2 of detail_evidence) is genuinely clearer than the prior "seasonally indeterminate" wording. Confidence rises from 0.88 to 0.92. Residual concerns: (1) analogous triple-counts phrasing in P3 of detail_verdict_text still reads self-referentially — ISS-1053 targeted only the finding field and missed the sibling phrasing; (2) the c5 advocate_mode defense_survives=3 has not been addressed; the queue reason cited EXP-167 as the neutralization, but EXP-167 is a tombstone for SEC-6.6 — no actual expansion exists for this defense. These are residual moderates, not blockers. The verdict Std Model Explains holds with high confidence.

citations_verified: Chapman 1933, Terrestrial Magnetism and Atmospheric Electricity 38:3 — standard historical reference (verified c5, no change)
Kim & Chang 2018, Advances in Space Research 61:2040-2054 — plausible ASR volume/pages (verified c5, no change)
citations_unchecked: Yamazaki & Maute 2021 Frontiers in Astronomy and Space Sciences 10.3389/fspas.2021.766327 — DOI format plausible but content not retrieved
Kim & Chang 2018 exact volume/page (61:2040-2054) — plausible but unverified against actual ASR 2018 index
Chernogor & Holub 2024 (cited in WIN-025 cross-reference; doi:10.1016/j.asr.2024.10.056) — out of scope for WIN-068 review but present via cross-reference

advocate_mode.best_defense: A careful dome defender says: "Your review admits (i) the observation is real, (ii) the aggregation is methodologically sound, (iii) the Chapman framework is semi-quantitative at best. You then attribute quantitative precision to "modern Sq models" — but those modern models incorporate empirically-measured ionospheric parameters (electron density, conductivity), so your "first principles" framing is itself post-hoc calibration by mainstream authors. The dome's FSF calibration differs from yours only in provenance. You also critique count inflation, but the three WINs DO differ in scope: WIN-010 is one station/one eclipse, WIN-025 is 9 stations/one eclipse, WIN-068 is 100 stations/39 eclipses. Ensemble generalization IS new evidence — that's why the dome cites all three. Finally, your refraction-cascade cross-reference assumes the dome mechanism requires physical sun altitude — but the verdict revert on 2026-04-13 itself established that eclipse magnetic effects track apparent geometry, so your own verdict logic undercuts that cross-reference." This defense has genuine force on points (i), (iii), and (iv). Point (ii) is weaker: the three WINs cite the same 1991-2016 INTERMAGNET dataset at different aggregation levels; WIN-025 (2024) is the only distinctly different event, so in effect the dome double-counts (WIN-010/068 both use ≤2016 data) rather than triple-counts in the strict sense.
advocate_mode.preemptive_recommendation: Three targeted additions would drop defense_survives to 0-1: (a) the Sq-parameters-independently-measured distinction (see hole 2 recommendation) neutralizes point (i); (b) the verdict-tension inoculation or detail_extra relocation (hole 3 option a/b) neutralizes point (iv); (c) phrasing harmonization (hole 1 recommendation) removes point (iii)'s rhetorical entry. Point (ii) about double-vs-triple count is worth investigating separately — the Kim & Chang 2018 dataset covers 1991-2016, which absorbs the 2017 BOU event cited in WIN-010 (actually Kim & Chang 2018 cutoff predates 2017, so no overlap — need to check event dates carefully). This is a c7 followup, not a c6 blocker.

cross_win_consistency.inconsistencies_found: WIN-010, WIN-025, and WIN-068 now have aligned Std Model Explains verdicts and aligned Chapman-mechanism primary arguments. All three reference the refraction-cascade cross-reference to Section 1.8. WIN-010 and WIN-025 use passive phrasing for count-inflation (WIN-010: "three of those claims share one mechanism"; WIN-025: "counting the same eclipse geomagnetic observation three times inflates the WIN count"). WIN-068 P3 uses active self-referential phrasing ("This WIN also triple-counts") which is slightly inconsistent with the sibling WINs — flagged in hole 1. WIN-012 (magnetic-gravity coupling 1.67 nT/uGal) uses the BOU 2017 -10.9 nT value from WIN-010 as an input, which is compatible because our critique of WIN-012 does not depend on whether -10.9 nT is dome-derived or standard-model-explained.

claims_checked: FSF range 0.642-2.075 -> ratio 2.075/0.642 = 3.232 -> text "3.2x spread" correct to 1 d.p.
Kim & Chang 2018 publication year vs V51.0 early 2026 = 8 years -> text "eight-year gap" correct
Paragraph count of detail_evidence = 4 (intro/cascade-xref/Chapman/triple-count-plus-Tier-1) matches expected structure
detail_verdict_length shrank from 1974 (c5) to 1958 (c6) = 16 char reduction, consistent with Meza citation removal ("; Meza et al. 2021" ~ 17 chars)

finding grew from 230 to 307 chars (+77), consistent with rewrite to drop self-reference and add "observational basis already counted as WIN-010 and WIN-025 — the ensemble is a larger aggregation" phrasing

H(r) = 8537 * exp(-r/8619): at r=20000, H ~ 8537 * exp(-2.32) = 8537 * 0.0983 = 839 km; at r=21000, H = 8537 * exp(-2.437) = 8537 * 0.0874 = 746 km; ratio 839/746 = 1.12 for 1000-km disc-radius difference. To produce FSF 3.2x requires ln(3.2) * 8619 = 10,000 km disc-radius spread - physically implausible for European stations. Arithmetic confirms our derivation critique.
quantitative_verification.computations: FSF ratio 2.075/0.642 = 3.232 OK. Year gap 2026-2018 = 8 OK. H(r) arithmetic: exp(-20000/8619) = 0.0983 so H(20000) ~ 839 km; exp(-21000/8619) = 0.0874 so H(21000) ~ 746 km. dH/H per 1000 km disc radius ~ 11.6%. For 3.2x FSF spread via H alone: delta_r = ln(3.2) * 8619 = 1.163 * 8619 = 10024 km - an order of magnitude beyond plausible inter-European variation.
quantitative_verification.discrepancies: None at c6. The eight-year gap is now correctly stated. All quantitative claims in the patched text are now internally consistent.
